steps.title: Identify the predicted crossover point
steps.content:
The passage states the original ranking “persisted except among the participants with high ignobility scores”—meaning the admirable-trait advantage holds for low ignobility participants, but reverses or disappears for high ignobility participants.

So the graph should show a crossover: admirable-trait candidates rated higher at low scores, but ignoble-trait candidates rated equal or higher at high scores.

steps.title: Match the pattern to the correct answer
steps.content:
The graph shows exactly this shift: at ignobility scores 1–5, the solid line (admirable-trait) stays above the dashed line (ignoble-trait); at scores 6–7, the dashed line rises above or meets the solid line.

The correct answer captures this threshold precisely—at 5 or less, admirable-trait candidates are more likable; at 6 or more, ignoble-trait candidates become equally or more likable.

The other choices either identify a correlation that reverses (choice A), claim the ratings don't change meaningfully (choice C), or misidentify where the shift occurs (choice D).

That confirms B is correct.
